ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific 'prawn masala' term; 'shrimp dish' (FOODON_03311737) is the closest defensible parent class. Prawn and shrimp are used interchangeably in food ontology contexts.
ontology.foodex2_description: Crustaceans, cooked — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for a cooked prawn-based dish with masala sauce.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Prawn Masala as a composite dish. A0CKP (Crustaceans, cooked) is used as the primary anchor. The masala sauce components (onion, tomato, spices) are not separately coded here.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Prawn Masala as a prepared dish. SNOMED CT coverage of composite ethnic dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single serving of Prawn Masala is approximately 200g, representing a moderate portion of prawns coated in thick masala sauce, typically served alongside rice or Indian bread such as roti or naan.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for Prawn Masala are adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' using prawn (cooked) and masala sauce component data. Cholesterol value cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for cooked shrimp (FDC ID 175180). Saturated fat and trans fat values are derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for cooked shrimp (FDC ID 175180) and adjusted for cooking oil in masala preparation. Trans fat is confirmed 0.0g per USDA data for this food type.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Prawn Masala in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) prawns are a high-protein, negligible-carbohydrate food with a very low GI contribution; (2) the masala sauce contains tomatoes, onions, and spices which contribute modest low-GI carbohydrates; (3) the overall dish carbohydrate content is 6g/100g, predominantly from vegetables and spices. This is consistent with the GI range reported for high-protein seafood dishes (GI 15–35) in peer-reviewed literature. Glycemic load per 200g serving = (25 × 6.0 × 2) / 100 = 3.0g, classified as low GL. Confidence: 0.7 (estimated).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 283mg per 100g (566mg per 200g serving) represents approximately 25% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000mg; the masala reduction process concentrates salt.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150g or less and request reduced salt during preparation, as the slow-cooked masala reduction significantly concentrates sodium; the potassium content (220mg/100g) provides partial cardiovascular benefit but does not offset high sodium intake at larger portions.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low estimated GI of 25 and high protein content (11.67g/100g) support glycemic control and satiety; carbohydrate content is modest at 6g/100g.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Prawn Masala is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in a standard 200g serving, as the low GI and high protein content help moderate postprandial blood glucose rise; however, total meal glycemic load should account for any accompanying rice or bread.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Dietary cholesterol is elevated at 119mg/100g (238mg per 200g serving), and sodium is high; however, saturated fat is relatively low at 1.67g/100g and the dish provides selenium (32mcg/100g) and vitamin B12 (1.8mcg/100g) which support cardiovascular health.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease may consume Prawn Masala in moderate portions (150–200g) as part of a heart-healthy diet, noting that the dietary cholesterol from prawns has less impact on serum LDL than saturated fat; sodium reduction during cooking is strongly advised, and the dish should not be consumed daily given cumulative sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Prawns provide omega-3 fatty acids; turmeric (curcumin), ginger, and garlic in the masala have documented anti-inflammatory properties.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Prawn Masala may be beneficial for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the anti-inflammatory properties of turmeric, ginger, and garlic in the masala base, combined with the omega-3 content of prawns; individuals should monitor personal tolerance to shellfish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Prawn Masala is a semi-dry, robustly flavored Indian seafood dish in which fresh prawns are slow-cooked in a thick, concentrated masala base made from caramelized onions, ripe tomatoes, and ginger-garlic paste. Unlike liquid curries, the dish is cooked until the gravy clings tightly to the prawns, intensifying all flavors through reduction. The spice blend typically includes cumin, coriander, turmeric, red chili, and garam masala, with the dish finished with a squeeze of lemon juice and fresh coriander leaves for brightness and freshness. The texture is tender-firm prawns coated in a deep, aromatic, slightly sticky sauce. Nutritionally, Prawn Masala provides approximately 140 kcal per 100g, with 11.67g of protein, 8.33g of fat, and 6g of carbohydrates, making it a high-protein, moderate-fat dish. It is a good source of selenium (32mcg/100g), vitamin B12 (1.8mcg/100g), phosphorus (160mg/100g), and potassium (220mg/100g). The dish has an estimated low glycemic index of approximately 25, making it suitable for blood sugar management. Sodium content is notable at 283mg/100g due to the masala reduction process, and dietary cholesterol from prawns is 119mg/100g. Clinically, it is appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes and is suitable in moderate portions for those with hypertension and cardiovascular disease if salt is controlled. It is halal-certified, gluten-free, and dairy-free. The dish is popular across coastal India — from Goa and Kerala in the south to Maharashtra and West Bengal — and is a staple at lunch and dinner, often served with steamed rice, roti, or naan. Regional variants include South Indian versions using curry leaves and mustard seeds, and Goan versions with coconut.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' for cooked prawn and masala sauce components, with cross-reference to USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for cooked shrimp. No single approved-source entry exists for Prawn Masala as a composite dish; values represent a defensible adaptation. GI is an analogous estimate (confidence 0.7) as no direct GI measurement for Prawn Masala exists in the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Prawn Masala as a composite dish is not available in ICMR-NIN or Singapore HPB database as of 2024; USDA FoodData Central reports MUFA ~0.5g and PUFA ~1.2g for plain cooked shrimp but these values are not directly applicable to the oil-enriched masala preparation without validated recipe-level calculation. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this semi-dry preparation is not reported in the primary source. 3. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0, confirmed consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for cooked crustaceans and vegetable-oil-based cooking; this is a confirmed zero for this food type. 4. Portions in the input record contained duplicates (three identical '1 serving (200g)' entries and two GrabFood entries); the canonical serving size of 200g has been adopted as the standard serving. 5. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Prawn Masala exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03311737 (shrimp dish) used as approximate parent; FoodEx2 A0CKP (Crustaceans, cooked) used as approximate anchor. No SNOMED CT code identified. 7. This is an Indian dish, not a Singaporean dish; Chinese, Malay multilingual fields are not mandatory and are left null. Hindi and Tamil names are provided per the input record and cultural context.
